text:
National Sock Mismatch Day falls on February 7th each year. The origins are murky, but most historians trace it to a laundry accident in 1987 that nobody bothered to correct.

The rules are simple. You wear two socks that do not match. That is the entire requirement. One striped, one polka dot. One wool, one athletic. One sock depicting a cartoon taco, one sock that is plain and beige and joyless. The contrast is the point.

Celebrations tend to be low-key. People post photos of their feet on social media with captions like "participating in the culture." Offices hold informal competitions for the most aggressively mismatched pair. The winner receives a gift card, usually for a coffee shop, usually in an amount too small to buy anything meaningful but large enough to create a moment of mild pleasure.

Some cities organize sock swaps. You bring a single sock. You leave with a different single sock. Nobody explains the logistics and nobody questions them.

Children enjoy the holiday most. It gives them permission to do something they were going to do anyway, and now it has a name. Teachers participate with visible reluctance. At least three parents send emails each year asking if this is an official school event. It is not. Nothing about this holiday is official.

The Sock Industry has attempted to capitalize on National Sock Mismatch Day by selling pre-mismatched sock sets. These are two socks, sold together, intentionally designed to not match. Critics argue this defeats the purpose. The Sock Industry does not care about critics.

By February 8th, everyone has moved on. The socks are back in the drawer. Order is restored. People return to wearing matching pairs as if nothing happened, slightly better for the experience.
